speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 3. Interview with [? Selma ?] Engel. You were telling us about your experiences in jail in Amsterdam. How long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was also about three months in jail in Amsterdam. And there was-- one-- what I told you, there was one woman who was very blonde, beautiful hair. And she said to me one day when we went [INAUDIBLE], you will be sent to Poland to find a husband. I was so insulted. I said, I don't have to go to-- to Poland to be-- to find a husband. And-- so after three months, we got-- we were in Christmastime there, so it was good. When-- in Christmastime, we got some extra food and extra different things. So-- so it was-- it was a good time to be in jail. And after three months, we-- and also in the morning, we were-- like you see in movies, [INAUDIBLE] and walked around. And it was a real jail. So in January, they-- they say to us, come in an office. And we went. We came into an office. All-- all the people were standing there that were in jail. All Jews. And somehow, we came together. Met a bunch of young girls. I think I was one of the oldest. I was in between-- now 20 years old. Yeah. And-- and all they were 16, 17, 18 years old, all young girls. And there I met some 15, 16 girls. And somehow, we stick together. Although, one was a German girl. And she was made a song that everything will go over. And perhaps one day, we'll see each other again. So something like that. But she knows already more what was going on, I think, than we Dutch people.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a German Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: A German Jewish girl. So from there, I went to a concentration camp in Holland by the Belgian border, Vught.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Vught?

speaker: interviewee
text: How we went there?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I absolutely don't think-- I don't think I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have-- I don't remember. I don't remember. We went to Vucht, V-U-C-H-T. And it was by the-- by the Belgian border, I told you. And when I came there, there was-- it was just opened. So there wasn't-- we didn't have blankets. It was in January. We didn't have blankets. There was no [INAUDIBLE] in the-- [INAUDIBLE]. There was not much food there. Because in Holland, there wasn't much food. The Germans really took everything out of the Holland, what they could get. And when I came, there was a man that came in our hotel. And he was already a little bit [INAUDIBLE] an organizer there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he say to me, would you like to be the head of the laundromat? And I-- and I say, yeah. Said-- said, what could happen? I didn't know what I-- what I had to do, but I was the head of the laundromat. And they had a whole bunch of people working there, and-- for the dirty clothes went in big baskets. I don't remember about the dirty clothes. I think they put it in themselves, in a big [INAUDIBLE] baskets. And that was sent away, and then it came back. And most of the stuff didn't come back. They stole. The Dutch stole. And everyone was very upset when it came back that we didn't have it. So the prisoners gave you their dirty clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. No, they did put it in somewhere. Because I don't remember that I sorted dirty clothes. I remember I sorted the clean clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your job, to sort the clean clothes when they came back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And give it back to the-- to all the people that-- where it was from. And-- so there was always a lot stole. And I could have gone out of that camp very easy. I could have gone in one of these big baskets and go out, but I didn't know where to go. And I told you before, I didn't have any money. And I thought, where will I go and what will I do? And then I thought, I better go to the family they already sent away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that I did three months. So I don't know-- I know that the men-- I don't know if the women had it very bad in that camp. I know the men in the men's department had it very, very bad. They shaved their heads. And they had very hard labor to do. And they were very undernourished, and they looked really [INAUDIBLE], really [? over ?] years in camp. Did you see the men?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the men. You could see them walking through the fence. And-- and also, when we went [INAUDIBLE], went to Westerbork in the train. That I know by train. But in camp, I saw them through the fence. I saw them through the fence. And it was-- I don't think the women, they had it that bad. Of course, it was bad we didn't have-- later on, we got blankets. But we didn't have blankets when we're getting in. There was not much food there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you sleep on?

speaker: interviewee
text: On benches, I think. I sleep-- the whole floor-- no, on beds. The whole floor, everything was [? standing ?] beds. So it was not like camp that is on the [? side, ?] but it was [INAUDIBLE]. We slept on-- on iron beds, like for what soldiers sleep on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many-- how often did they feed you each day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I think there was just normal three days a day. I don't remember that I had hungry there. I remember in jail, I had hungry. But not-- I don't remember there I was hungry there. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who guarded the camp? Who-- who was in charge of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older Dutch people. I remember that woman that I showed to you, it was in jail. She [? went over with ?] one of the heads, walked around with him. She was very beautiful, so she-- so-- but I don't remember that. I don't know. It was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Dutch guards you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, most was Dutch guards. Dutch were very Nazi-oriented in Holland. 75% from the Dutch people were Nazis in Holland. No, the largest percent-- 75% from the Dutch Jews were killed. The large-- after Germany and Austria, the largest percentage from people who were Nazis was in Holland. [INAUDIBLE] told [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So the camp was run by the Dutch German sympathizers you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they treat--

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were Germans, too, of course. [? It went out ?] [? from the ?] Germans. I don't-- I don't know if we were-- it was a very difficult time. It was very-- all people were there. And we had all to sit together in the-- in the bathroom. And it was a [INAUDIBLE] bathrooms. It was a very hard time. We were young and-- and unexperienced for life. But for all the people to sit-- I mean, all these young kids and take showers with all the kids-- young kids when they said we could take showers. And I don't remember that either. But of course, we could wash ourselves there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have hot water do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I think so. I think so. I don't remember things like that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a day did you have to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that either. We had-- we had full days. We were-- long days. And there was not much going on. And then we went to bed, right away to bed. There was no entertainment of anything, like in other camps in Holland. We had-- I had-- we had to go. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people talk to each other about what was happening to them? What they thought was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a word. No. No. What happening that-- I don't think that we Dutch people know anything what was going on. We had no radios. And it was very dangerous to use a radio. So when you had a radio, you have a chance that-- you-- they take it right away, away. So we didn't know that we go to a camp. [INAUDIBLE] do in a camp? You go work in a camp. You will go out eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you stayed at Vught for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: There I stayed also three months till April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, we were sent to Westerbork. Westerbork is a concentration camp in Holland where all the Dutch Jews went through. So after the war, they could find out exactly what time and when and how and where they were sent, all the Jews. And that was a concentration camp made for the German Jews. A camp for the German Jews. It wasn't really a concentration camp. Yeah, it was concentrated that the Jews were there, but that wasn't all. But it was a very nice-made village-- [? where a ?] cabaret and a-- and a little cafe. And it was everything for the German Jews. And they were the leaders there.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were still there when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were still there. And-- when I came to Westerbork, I had a brother from my mother there. Met three little boys and an aunt. And they want to get me out. I was punished because I was hidden, so I couldn't get out. And you could stay in Westerbork. Everybody was very happy, but that was an-- an-- a good life there. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: There's something I don't understand. Your aunt was punished because--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was punished.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were punished--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was hidden. And they caught me by the police. And that's the reason I had no chance to come out and stay in Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: Westerbork was a [INAUDIBLE]. It was not a camp where people stayed. Only the German Jews and sometimes people that had a little bit connection with the Jews [INAUDIBLE] or with something else, they could stay there, you know? And it was in Westerbork, you were safe. But you were told you couldn't stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They told me that I couldn't come out of the-- the barrack, out of the place where I stayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, oh, I see. That you had to stay in the barrack that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the barrack. Have to stay in the barrack because I was punished because I was caught by the police, and I was hidden. How-- who ran that camp? Who-- who was in charge of that camp? Germans? Dutch? I think Germans-- Germans were. I think German Jews and Germans. Of course, Germans. When Germans make the decision-- oh, definitely Germans. Now I remember. All Germans [? by ?] train when we went to [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah, the Germans were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody who went-- sent to Poland went to Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. I remember I was sitting in a train. Met a young man and girl. And I remember they-- they come from my hometown. And he looked terrible. He looked terrible. And there was a very well-educated, nice, good-looking boy. And he looked unbelievable. And he was already-- and there was in Vught. This happened in Vught. That's the reason I know the men suffered a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he talk about-- did you talk on the train about what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we talked about it, how he suffered. But he was there with his girlfriend. What was-- and-- and he want just to talk with her, really. You know? And he didn't see her a long time, and they want to just talk. So-- so we didn't talk much about-- see, it was always-- you didn't want to talk much. You never knew who was in the next train and who could listen to you. So you didn't talk in public so much about what happened. What-- what-- what-- you know, what you were afraid of. Only your own people can be a-- a traitor. You don't know. You were-- you were afraid to talk much about things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Westerbork, were you surprised at what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see much of it. They lock-- they locked us up in a barrack, and that was it. I didn't see anything from Westerbork myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the barrack like at Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big barrack. A bunch of beds on top of each other. And there we slept. And we stayed together, met this-- and somehow in Westerbork, met these girls that I met in jail. And also, Vught. Somehow, we stayed together. And that's very important. I think saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when we came in Sobibor, we worked-- all these Germans were standing there and the one [? French ?] soldier [? used ?] to say, come out. And we were allowed to go out. We were all young, pretty, healthy-looking girls. And [? Viva ?] used to say, go and stay on the side. And that, I think, is the reason I got saved. Yeah, that saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long. A week. One week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have anything to do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were sitting on top of our beds. No. We didn't-- it was-- no. No. We didn't have anything to do. We were just laying on bed and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you learn about where you were going next?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came on the Sobibor. I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: That they--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they got you out of the bed. Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Got us out of the-- out of the barrack and give us, I think, some water. I don't know for sure. And put us in a train with [? 40 ?] people. I think all 60 people. And a little bit straw on the floor. And there was one barrel in the corner that was the bathroom. But old people, young people, and-- in a freight train. Not in a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there room to sit down?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on the floor, yeah. We could sit, I think. And was-- and then we went three days and three nights. And the Germans were shooting. They stopped many times and were shooting over and on the side. And-- and dogs around. And-- when we could have a chance, and everybody also looks through his little window. And saw the people like [? did ?] to us. And we thought they hate us so much, they want to have us dead. And they want to tell us that we go to our death. And that was already in Poland, of course, when they stopped. And I thought we were in Russia. Everybody looked so poor and different than in Holland, the farmers looking. So that was a terrible trip. We didn't have much-- we Didn't have anything to eat. And it was hot. And-- and-- it was-- it was terrible. But I was young. And when you're young, you can do a lot. So I didn't suffer so much as the older people. They suffered much more than we did. And going out-- out in public to go to the bathroom and things like that, I mean, it was-- it was-- it was horrible. But we were young. And when you're young, you can do a lot of things and when-- than when you're older.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after three days and three nights, stopping many times and shooting all over, like we're going to run away. How could we ever come out? A door with a piece of iron over it. We-- but to make us scared [INAUDIBLE]. If we-- So finally, we come out in Sobibor. And you could see the train was written at top was Sobibor. And we came out and it looks very pretty. The windows were-- were little geraniums and people were working there. And flowers and little curtains. And there where the Germans slept. So we didn't know, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the minute they opened these doors, we saw all people in blue uniform. And that is the-- the train commander. It was special train commander. And they have whip. And they-- we are rolling over each other. [INAUDIBLE]. And the screaming and the screaming. And we're falling over top of each other. We're falling over each other, almost. And we had to go and work. And it was a little train, a little train and a trolley that goes like that. And there, they put in children that got lost from the parents. And-- and also, older people that couldn't work. They put it in there. And that train went right straight to the gas chamber. They didn't take even time to take the clothes off. And we had to work. And it was overhang something. And there we had to throw away our backpack. And one woman throwed away her backpack and her baby fell. And-- and I-- I-- I see [INAUDIBLE] happened today. And I had it also written. I have a little dairy, written in my diary, that she throw it away. And she say, oh, my baby. My baby. And she want to take the baby and the German, which was standing there, hit her. And the blood was streaming from her face. And he say, we will take care of the baby. But they did, of course. They put it also in the trailer and go straight to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, we walk. And then we passed all the SS's. I saw the movie that you could see it. They were really standing all there. And we passed together, met these Dutch girls. Always, somehow we stick together. But we didn't want to be, perhaps, with all the people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said-- they say to us-- there was the first transport where the two Dutch girls are. There weren't men then. Dutch men were already taken out. And say, go stay on the side. And we stay on the side. And I saw the women and the children passing us. And they done went straight to the gas chamber. That was on the left side. And they walked through a fence. And then the men had to sit over there. And they had to take their shoes off. And they had to put the shoes together with laces. And-- when they-- you saw Germans speaking to them. Later, I found out what they say. They say to them, we are here in Sobibor, and everything is fine. And tell your family that you're doing well. You will go now to a camp and work there. And you will take a shower first. You [? wear ?] your clean-- clean clothes. And right home. So-- so-- and then I saw that. And then, we were in between-- they brought-- when I saw the German speaking, they brought us to camp one. Sobibor was divided in three camps. Camp one-- camp one was where we slept. Camp two was where we-- where we worked. And camp three, that was the gas chamber. There was [? no ?] camp four, what I heard later, where the clothes-- they called it also where the clothes was from people that died in the gas chamber. So camp three was the death camp. And we know-- we never looked at that. That was just the death. So we came on in camp one with all the girls together. And I saw some people from my hometown. Three boys. And there was two very close. [? Vaughn ?] was-- his parents were my parents' best friend. And the other one stayed in our house when the German took his house. And he stayed with us-- with my father in the hotel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I say hi. How are you? And they didn't even say hello. They didn't say anything. And so strange. They know me very well. So then they went away to work and then we went to work. What did camp one look like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp one was looked like-- I thought in that time-- like, I saw movies once from Mexico, from [INAUDIBLE]. It was all wooden. And Holland's [INAUDIBLE] stone. And there was all wooden barracks all around, so all around. And there we slept. It was bed, a bathroom. There was a barrack for men and there was a kitchen. And there was a barrack for women. And there was a place for the-- for ironing and one for the laundry. And one was for the goldsmith that worked at a little place. And for the carpenter also for the German. Carpenter. And there was a-- and a shoemaker. And there was something else on the-- on the back. [? Copper? ?] Oh, and somebody that painted. There was very famous painter-- [? Van Damme ?] was his name-- from Holland. And he painted for the Germans. So that was all around us. And then, you came-- we didn't go to our barrack, not to our place where we slept. So we're standing there, and then they brought us to camp one-- to camp two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these German guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know, those kapos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kapos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are the kapos. And then they brought us to camp two. And when we came on-- in camp two, we had to sort clothes. Were long tables and a few girls standing around it. And there were all suitcases on the floor. All suitcases on the floor. And there, we had to put first quality, second quality. We had to take papers out in all the-- in the pockets and under the shoulders. And-- and-- that-- you know, and-- And then also also the-- with that was-- the papers were burned outside. And the man that burned it was a friend of Chaim. So all-- all first quality, second quality. All the pants together and blouses together and-- and all separate. That was from the people that were already dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no idea what I was doing. Absolutely no idea. We got, from the Dutch people, is a lot of food, Dutch food, and-- and nice silk stockings, and-- and everything. So-- and we-- I had no idea what I was doing. It was-- and when it was finished, we come out. And it was already a little bit dark. And we went on roll call. And then we walked to camp one. And every time that we walked, we had to sing stories. Had to sing for the Germans. And many times, we had to-- later on, we had to lay down on the floor, and going up, and laying down on the floor, until we met a whip hitting us. And there was-- there was always punishment. They had always some kind of a punishment for us after we came back from work.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very dangerous to do anything wrong because were all Germans standing there, too. And also, I tried to sabotage always. I-- when I did it, I cut always everything up. Chaim say to me, [? Selma, ?] you know how dangerous it was later on? And I-- I just still did it, cutting-- What-- what did you do? I cut up nice blouses with a scissor or anything that was nice that I could cut up, I did. So that the Germans didn't get it. When I know later on, already that everything went to Germany. Right, now this--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very dangerous to do things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure. This first day, did they give you anything to eat when you got back to camp one?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- who was showing you where to sleep? Where to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on. So then we were on roll call, and we walked to camp one. When we came in camp one, there was already a big fire burning from the people. And it smelled from hair and bone. I had-- I didn't know what it was. I had no idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came in camp one, and all the Germans were standing there, the SS's. And there were a few people that had instruments. And they took that with them. And they had to play for the Germans, the enjoyment from the first-- second-- you know, something against-- I hated it again that we had to dance and play music and the fire was burning. So he-- they told us to dance. And we had to dance, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Chaim had to dance for the Germans. And he asked me to dance. And [INAUDIBLE] that's the way I met Chaim. When I speak in schools, I say always, and he fell instantly in love with me. And everybody laughs. And I feel happy that somebody laughs on that moment. So after that, I saw these three boys that I know from hometown. And they say-- told us where we sleep, I think. When I don't-- not somebody officially tells you where you sleep. If the girls are all [INAUDIBLE], they tell you where you sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told us, do you know what that fire is? And see, that's my-- my wife a few months ago, or a few weeks ago. [? Van Damme, ?] one of the-- he had a baby from nine months old. And the other one had a baby from two years old, that his wife went already there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, when you come to a place like that, you cannot-- you cannot understand that there is such a thing, that all these people when you came, they are now burning, and that they are dead, and that you were sorting their clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I don't think that-- that came through my-- that I could understand what was going on. And so, I-- I tell you, it took me a long, long time to understand that I was in a death camp and that I was alive. Because how come that these people are not alive? You know? I-- what was the-- what was my luck that I could do all these things? And I've-- and I-- that they let me live and these people-- it is something that you couldn't understand. So when I came there, we-- we slept in these barracks, like you saw in pictures-- on the three row high. I slept in the middle. And one of the girls, [? Ula ?] [INAUDIBLE]-- that also survived Sobibor-- she was born in Germany-- slept next to me. And you have always in camp a girl that-- that is your buddy. When you are very sick or something like that, that helps you and things like that. So she-- we were good-- friendly because we came-- know each other three days. And it was terrible. There was a barrel-- a pail inside that was the bathroom.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a bathroom far away. And it was just a hole where you went to the bathroom. And there-- and we got in the morning-- when we stand up, we didn't get any-- you know, it was so dirty there. It was just-- the people that slept there already, they tried to hold their self clean. And they looked clean because you could take the clothes-- you could steal clothes from the people that went to the gas chamber. So outside, you looked clean, but you was-- there was lice and-- and-- I don't know how you call these animals. In bed-- bed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bed bugs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bed bugs. It was-- it was just unbelievable. And the-- the-- the blankets and everything was just-- it was-- we were very, very dirty because we couldn't wash our self the first month. No, two months. And later on, we got once a week a little pail with water that we can wash yourself a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: We've come to the end of this tape.
